stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 with a confirmed MCDA twin pregnancy at 19+5 weeks attends fortnightly surveillance. The 16-week scan was unremarkable. Today's ultrasound: twin 1 (donor) EFW 22nd centile, DVP 1.6 cm, bladder not seen on three sweeps over 30 minutes; twin 2 (recipient) EFW 56th centile, DVP 9.2 cm, distended bladder, normal MCA-PSV. Umbilical artery Dopplers normal both twins, no foetal hydrops, cervix 38 mm. She has no contractions, no bleeding, no symptoms of pre-eclampsia. The tertiary foetal medicine unit is 2 hours away and offers fetoscopic laser ablation daily. She wants the option that maximises the chance of two healthy survivors.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender uterus appropriate for 24 weeks size
data_table.Investigations: Twin 1: EFW 22nd centile, DVP 1.6 cm, bladder not seen. Twin 2: EFW 56th centile, DVP 9.2 cm, bladder distended, MCA-PSV normal. UA Dopplers normal both twins. Cervix 38 mm closed.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Urgent referral to a tertiary foetal medicine unit for fetoscopic laser ablation of the placental anastomoses
options.rationale: She has stage I/II TTTS (Quintero stage II given absent donor bladder, with oligohydramnios <2 cm vs polyhydramnios >8 cm; normal Dopplers excluding stage III; no hydrops). Fetoscopic laser ablation between 16-26 weeks gives the best dual-survival outcome and is recommended over amnioreduction (RANZCOG twins; ISUOG).

options.text: Reassure and continue routine fortnightly surveillance for 2 more weeks
options.rationale: Reassurance and fortnightly surveillance is wrong because untreated Quintero stage II TTTS has high perinatal mortality; the anti-pattern is waiting for progression. Would be correct only for Stage I with stable Dopplers under close specialist review, not Stage II (RANZCOG).

options.text: Amnioreduction of the recipient sac in the local maternity unit
options.rationale: Amnioreduction is wrong because the Eurofoetus and subsequent trials showed fetoscopic laser ablation gives superior dual-survival and lower neurological morbidity than amnioreduction in stage II-IV TTTS at 16-26 weeks. Would be acceptable only if laser is unavailable, the woman declines transfer, or gestation is outside the laser window (RANZCOG; ISUOG).

options.text: Admit for antenatal corticosteroids and plan delivery this week
options.rationale: Antenatal corticosteroids and delivery at 19+5 is wrong because 19+5 weeks is pre-viable; delivery would result in dual neonatal loss. Would be correct only at periviable gestations after 24 weeks with stage III-IV TTTS refractory to laser (RANZCOG).

options.text: Selective foetal reduction of the donor twin by cord occlusion now
options.rationale: Cord occlusion of the donor is wrong because selective reduction by cord occlusion is reserved for Stage IV-V or when laser has failed/is not feasible (e.g. severe hydrops in one twin, anterior placenta with poor access); at Stage II laser is first line. Would be correct only as salvage therapy when laser is unsuitable (RANZCOG; ISUOG).

explanation.summary: TTTS complicates around 10 to 15 percent of MCDA pregnancies and is diagnosed by discordant amniotic fluid (DVP under 2 and over 8 cm) before 20 weeks. Fetoscopic laser ablation of placental anastomoses is first-line.
key_points: Fortnightly TVS and US from 16 weeks for MCDA (RANZCOG)
Quintero staging: I fluid only; II absent bladder donor; III abnormal Dopplers; IV hydrops; V demise
Laser ablation reduces perinatal death and neurological injury vs amnioreduction
explanation.pearls: DVP, not AFI, is the standard discordance measure in twins.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy
NICE NG137: Twin and triplet pregnancy

stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 with an MCDA twin pregnancy is reviewed at 26 weeks. Booking, anatomy scan, and TTTS surveillance to date have been unremarkable. Today the smaller twin has an EFW on the 4th centile with a 28 percent inter-twin weight discordance. The larger twin tracks the 48th centile. Amniotic fluid is normal in both sacs. Umbilical artery Doppler of the smaller twin shows intermittently absent end-diastolic flow that alternates with normal forward flow within a single tracing; the larger twin's UA Doppler is normal. Both ductus venosus a-waves are positive. The cardiotocograph of both twins is reactive. The woman is well with a closed cervix of 34 mm.

data_table.Investigations: Smaller twin EFW 4th centile, discordance 28%, UA Doppler intermittent AEDF (Type III). Larger twin EFW 48th centile, UA Doppler normal. DV a-waves positive both. CTG reactive both. Cervix 34 mm.

lead_in: Which classification and surveillance plan best fits this pattern?

options.text: Type I sIUGR; fortnightly growth scans, aim for planned delivery at 36 weeks
options.rationale: Type I labelling is wrong because Gratacos Type I has persistently positive UA end-diastolic flow with a favourable prognosis; intermittent absent end-diastolic flow defines Type III. Would be correct only if UA Dopplers remained continuously positive (Gratacos sIUGR classification).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

options.text: Type II sIUGR; weekly Doppler and CTG surveillance, plan delivery at 32 weeks
options.rationale: Type II labelling is wrong because Type II shows persistently absent or reversed UA flow with progressive deterioration and earlier delivery (often 28-30 weeks); the intermittent pattern here is Type III. Would be correct only if AEDF or REDF were persistent across studies (Gratacos sIUGR classification).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

options.text: Type III sIUGR; twice-weekly Doppler with corticosteroid cover and delivery at 32 to 34 weeks
options.rationale: Intermittent AEDF in the smaller MCDA twin defines Type III, which carries unpredictable risk of sudden co-twin demise from large anastomoses; close surveillance and planned preterm delivery are recommended.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

options.text: Discordant growth without sIUGR; reassure the woman and re-scan in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Reassuring as benign discordance is wrong because EFW below the 10th centile in either twin combined with greater than 25% discordance meets the consensus definition of sIUGR; the anti-pattern is treating this as physiological variation. Would be correct only if both twins were AGA with milder discordance, not here (ISUOG).

options.text: Stage I twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome; refer urgently for fetoscopic laser ablation now
options.rationale: TTTS labelling is wrong because TTTS requires discordant fluid (DVP <2 cm in one and >8 cm in the other); fluid is normal in both sacs, so the pathology is sIUGR not TTTS. Would be correct only if oligohydramnios-polyhydramnios were present (RANZCOG; ISUOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

explanation.summary: sIUGR in MCDA twins is classified by UA Doppler: Type I positive EDF, Type II persistent AEDF or REDF, Type III intermittent AEDF. Type III carries a particular risk of unexpected single-twin demise.
key_points: Definition: EFW under 10th centile in one twin or inter-twin discordance over 25% (ISUOG)
MCDA Type III: large arterio-arterial anastomoses cause unpredictable shunt reversal
Plan delivery 32 to 34 weeks with corticosteroids per RANZCOG
explanation.pearls: Co-twin demise risk and neurological injury risk rise sharply in MCDA Type III sIUGR.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

stem: A 30-year-old G1P0 RhD-negative woman with a DCDA twin pregnancy (two viable embryos at 7+1) re-presents at 13+0 with a single episode of pink spotting yesterday, now resolved. She is haemodynamically stable. Ultrasound today: twin A viable, CRL appropriate for 13+0; twin B sac collapsed, embryo CRL 7+5 with no cardiac activity (demised). Antibody screen at booking and today: negative. She has not previously received anti-D in this pregnancy. She is otherwise well.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, closed cervical os, scant brown discharge
data_table.Investigations: TVS: twin A viable, twin B demised (CRL 7+5, no FH). Blood group O Rh-positive.

lead_in: Which combination of management is most appropriate?

options.text: Reassure regarding minimal increased risk to the surviving twin from the early loss; administer anti-D 625 IU IM as the bleeding episode is at 13+0; continue routine DCDA twin surveillance
options.rationale: Early vanishing twin (before 14 weeks) confers minimal increased risk to the surviving co-twin. As she is now 13+0 with a sensitising event, anti-D 625 IU IM is required (NBA gives 625 IU from 13 weeks). DCDA surveillance continues 4-weekly from 24 weeks (NBA; RANZCOG).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

options.text: Administer anti-D 250 IU IM as the dose for sensitising events before 20 weeks
options.rationale: Anti-D 250 IU is wrong because the NBA dose increases to 625 IU IM from 13+0 weeks for sensitising events; 250 IU is the pre-12-week dose. Would be correct only before 13 weeks, not at exactly 13+0 (National Blood Authority Anti-D guidelines).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

options.text: Withhold anti-D until 28 weeks routine prophylaxis as the antibody screen is negative
options.rationale: Withholding anti-D until 28 weeks is wrong because sensitising events require anti-D within 72 hours regardless of negative antibody screen or upcoming routine prophylaxis; waiting risks sensitisation. Would be correct only for a non-sensitising event with no bleeding (National Blood Authority).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

options.text: Surgical evacuation of the demised gestational sac to prevent later miscarriage of the surviving twin
options.rationale: Surgical evacuation is wrong because vanishing twins resorb naturally and instrumentation risks loss of the surviving twin from membrane disruption or infection. Would be correct only if there were septic retained products threatening the surviving pregnancy, which is not described (RANZCOG).

options.text: Admit for serial coagulation studies given the increased maternal risk of disseminated intravascular coagulation
options.rationale: Serial coagulation studies for DIC is wrong because maternal DIC risk applies to single-twin demise after viability (typically >=20 weeks) with prolonged in-utero retention; first-trimester vanishing twin does not generate the necessary thromboplastin load. Would be correct only for second/third-trimester co-twin demise (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Vanishing twin in a DCDA pregnancy diagnosed in the first trimester is associated with a marginally higher singleton miscarriage rate but generally a good prognosis for the surviving foetus. Risk is considerably higher in monochorionic pregnancies due to shared circulation.
key_points: DCDA vanishing twin in T1: routine antenatal care, no specific intervention
MC vanishing twin: risk of neurological injury or co-twin demise via shared anastomoses
Anti-D only if Rh-negative per NBA guidance
explanation.pearls: Always re-check chorionicity from the original dating scan when one twin is lost.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy
RANZCOG: Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing for genetic conditions

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 with a confirmed MCMA twin pregnancy is reviewed at 31+4. Booking confirmed a single placental mass, no inter-twin membrane on serial scans, and entangled cords on colour Doppler. Growth has tracked the 35th to 45th centiles concordantly, Dopplers are normal, and there is no evidence of TTTS. She has been an inpatient since 26 weeks for daily CTG monitoring. Both twins are presently non-cephalic on today's presentation scan. The cervix is closed at 32 mm on TVS. She received a complete course of antenatal corticosteroids at 26 weeks and an additional rescue course was given two weeks ago. She has received daily inpatient CTG surveillance without abnormal traces, no episodes of bleeding or rupture of membranes, and a recent maternal infection screen is unremarkable. The neonatal unit on site routinely supports infants from 28 weeks. She is keen to proceed with planned delivery once the team agrees timing.

data_table.Examination: Inpatient, comfortable, daily CTG reactive for both twins
data_table.Investigations: Concordant growth, normal Dopplers, no TTTS, both non-cephalic, cervix 32 mm

lead_in: Which delivery plan is most appropriate?

options.text: Planned caesarean between 32+0 and 34+0 weeks after corticosteroids
options.rationale: MCMA twins are recommended to deliver by planned caesarean at 32+0 to 34+0 weeks due to risk of cord entanglement and intrauterine death rising sharply after 32 weeks (RANZCOG, KEMH).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

options.text: Planned caesarean at 36+0 to 36+6 after steroids
options.rationale: 36+0 to 36+6 is wrong because that is the MCDA window; in MCMA, cord entanglement risk rises after 32 weeks and waiting to 36 is unsafe. Would be correct for MCDA, not MCMA (RANZCOG; KEMH).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

options.text: Planned vaginal birth at 32+0 to 34+0 with continuous CTG
options.rationale: Vaginal birth with CTG is wrong because cord entanglement in a shared sac makes labour intrinsically high-risk regardless of presentation; the anti-pattern is treating MCMA like MCDA. Would never be correct in MCMA, even with cephalic-cephalic presentation (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

options.text: Continue inpatient monitoring and aim for vaginal birth at 37+0
options.rationale: Aiming for 37+0 vaginally is wrong because it stacks the worst of both errors: extending well past the MCMA safety window plus exposing twins to labour-related entanglement risk. Would never be correct in MCMA (RANZCOG; KEMH).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

options.text: Immediate caesarean today at 31+4 regardless of stability
options.rationale: Immediate caesarean at 31+4 is wrong because without a deterioration trigger (abnormal CTG, sIUGR progression, TTTS), birth at 31+4 imposes prematurity morbidity that the 32-34 week window is designed to avoid. Would be correct only with evidence of foetal compromise or maternal indication (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

explanation.summary: MCMA twins share a single amniotic sac with high cord entanglement risk. Inpatient surveillance from around 26 weeks and planned caesarean at 32+0 to 34+0 after corticosteroids is the Australian standard.
key_points: MCMA delivery: planned caesarean 32+0 to 34+0 (RANZCOG, KEMH)
Inpatient daily CTG often offered from 26 to 28 weeks
Cord entanglement risk underlies the early delivery decision
explanation.pearls: MCMA is rare (around 1 percent of MC twins) but high-risk; centralise care.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

subtopic_detail: Mode and timing of delivery - MCMA twins

stem: A 25-year-old G1P0 at 28+3 presents to the birth suite with regular painful contractions every 4 minutes for the past 2 hours. She has had no bleeding, no rupture of membranes, and no fever. Her booking and pregnancy have been uncomplicated. She has well-controlled chronic hypertension on labetalol 200 mg BD with booking BP averaging 130/82 and today 134/86. She has known mild aortic stenosis (peak gradient 22 mmHg, asymptomatic) and is followed by cardiology. Examination shows a soft tender uterus with palpable tightenings, cephalic presentation, and the cervix is 2 cm dilated and 50 percent effaced on speculum. Foetal heart is reassuring. Foetal fibronectin is not available at this hour. The plan is to give corticosteroids and MgSO4 for neuroprotection and transfer to a tertiary unit.

data_table.Examination: Uterus contracting q4min, cephalic, cervix 2 cm 50% effaced, membranes intact
data_table.Investigations: Foetal heart reassuring, MSU pending, FBC and UEC normal

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate tocolytic to commence while corticosteroids and transfer are organised?

options.text: Oral nifedipine immediate-release as calcium-channel blocker tocolysis now
options.rationale: Nifedipine is wrong because Therapeutic Guidelines flag a relative contraindication when co-prescribed with antihypertensives like labetalol due to additive hypotension and risk of uteroplacental compromise. Would be correct only in women not on antihypertensives without cardiovascular concerns (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics).

options.text: Intravenous atosiban infusion as oxytocin-receptor antagonist tocolysis now
options.rationale: Atosiban is the preferred tocolytic when there are maternal cardiovascular concerns, is effective and has a favourable maternal safety profile vs nifedipine, and avoids the hypotensive interaction with antihypertensives.

options.text: Oral indomethacin 50 mg loading dose then 25 mg six-hourly
options.rationale: Indomethacin is wrong because it is generally avoided from around 32 weeks due to foetal ductus arteriosus constriction and oligohydramnios; even though 28+3 is technically within window, atosiban has a better maternal safety profile here. Would be a reasonable alternative under 32 weeks if atosiban is unavailable (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics).

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate infusion used primarily as tocolysis
options.rationale: Magnesium sulfate as tocolysis is wrong because magnesium has been shown ineffective as a tocolytic and is instead reserved for foetal neuroprotection at <30 weeks. Would be correct only as neuroprotection (the question separately requires this), not tocolysis (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection of the fetus
Cochrane: Magnesium for preventing preterm birth

options.text: Intravenous salbutamol infusion as beta-2 agonist tocolysis now
options.rationale: IV salbutamol is wrong because beta-agonists are no longer recommended in Australia for tocolysis (maternal tachycardia, pulmonary oedema, hyperglycaemia, hypokalaemia); they have been withdrawn from first-line practice. Would never be first-line in current Australian guidance (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics).

explanation.summary: Tocolysis aims to delay birth for 48 hours to allow corticosteroid effect and in-utero transfer. Nifedipine and atosiban are equally effective overall, but choice depends on maternal comorbidity. Atosiban is preferred over nifedipine where there are cardiovascular concerns or concurrent antihypertensives.
key_points: First-line: nifedipine or atosiban (RANZCOG, eTG)
Atosiban preferred where antihypertensives, cardiac disease, or hypovolaemia
Indomethacin: avoid after 32 weeks (ductal constriction)
Beta-agonists no longer routinely used
explanation.pearls: Tocolysis is only justified when corticosteroids or transfer will meaningfully change neonatal outcome.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection of the fetus
Cochrane: Magnesium for preventing preterm birth

stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 at 33+1 presents with painful tightenings every 6 minutes for 4 hours. She had a previous preterm birth at 31 weeks. She has no rupture of membranes or bleeding. Examination shows a cephalic presentation, cervix 1 cm dilated and 30 percent effaced; CTG is reactive. She has no maternal medical comorbidities and is on no regular medication. Her registrar suggests indomethacin tocolysis to allow corticosteroid effect, citing its rapid onset. She has no rupture of membranes, no antepartum haemorrhage and no fever. Maternal observations are normal, urine dipstick is clear and the foetal heart on continuous CTG is reactive with normal variability. Booking scans were unremarkable and a recent foetal anatomy review at 31 weeks was normal. The local maternity unit has nifedipine, atosiban, indomethacin and magnesium sulfate available. The team is balancing tocolytic options to allow corticosteroids to take effect, and the registrar has specifically asked about indomethacin given its rapid onset.

data_table.Investigations: CTG reactive, FBC and UEC normal

lead_in: Which is the strongest reason to avoid indomethacin in this patient?

options.text: Risk of foetal ductus arteriosus constriction and oligohydramnios at this advanced gestation
options.rationale: Indomethacin is generally avoided from around 32 weeks because of dose-dependent foetal ductal constriction and oligohydramnios; nifedipine or atosiban is preferred at 33+1.

options.text: Substantially increased risk of maternal pulmonary oedema compared with other tocolytics
options.rationale: Pulmonary oedema is wrong because that adverse profile belongs to beta-agonists (and prolonged combination tocolysis), not indomethacin; the question targets indomethacin-specific harm at 33+1. Would point to salbutamol or atosiban concerns instead (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics).

options.text: Indomethacin causes significant maternal hypotension via systemic vasodilation in pregnancy
options.rationale: Maternal hypotension is wrong because vasodilator hypotension is a calcium channel blocker (nifedipine) effect, not an indomethacin effect; indomethacin acts on prostaglandin synthesis. Would describe nifedipine, not indomethacin (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics).

options.text: Indomethacin is absolutely contraindicated whenever there is any prior preterm birth
options.rationale: Prior preterm birth as absolute contraindication is wrong because prior preterm birth is the indication for treatment, not a contraindication; gestational age and renal/GI status are the relevant flags. Would never be a valid contraindication (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics).

options.text: Indomethacin significantly reduces lung-maturation efficacy of antenatal corticosteroids in pregnancy
options.rationale: Reduced steroid efficacy is wrong because there is no evidence indomethacin attenuates betamethasone lung maturation effect; the late-gestation concern is foetal ductus and oligohydramnios. Would not be a recognised concern (Therapeutic Guidelines: Obstetrics).

explanation.summary: Indomethacin is an option for tocolysis at very preterm gestations but is generally avoided after about 32 weeks due to risk of ductus arteriosus constriction and oligohydramnios.
key_points: Indomethacin upper limit ~32 weeks (RANZCOG, eTG)
Nifedipine and atosiban are alternatives 32+ weeks
Foetal renal and ductal effects are dose- and duration-dependent
explanation.pearls: Always document gestation when prescribing indomethacin for tocolysis.

stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 at 31+5 presents with threatened preterm labour. She had a complete course of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM two doses 24 hours apart at 26+0 for an earlier admission with threatened preterm labour that subsequently settled. Today she is contracting every 3 minutes, has a cervix 3 cm dilated and 70 percent effaced on speculum, and a foetal fibronectin from 4 weeks ago was positive. The CTG is reactive. The team plans to start tocolysis, transfer to a tertiary unit, and give magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection.

data_table.Examination: Cervix 3 cm/70% effaced, cephalic, contractions q3min
data_table.Investigations: CTG reactive, FBC and UEC normal

lead_in: Regarding antenatal corticosteroids, which is most appropriate now?

options.text: Give a single rescue dose of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM now
options.rationale: RANZCOG supports a single rescue dose when the initial course was given over 7 days ago, gestation is under 34+0, and birth is anticipated within the next 7 days.

options.text: Give a full repeat course of betamethasone (two doses 24 h apart)
options.rationale: Full repeat course is wrong because Australian guidance favours a single rescue dose rather than a full repeat; multiple repeat courses are associated with reduced birth weight and head circumference. Would be correct only in some international protocols, not RANZCOG (RANZCOG).

options.text: Do not give further corticosteroids as one course is sufficient lifetime exposure
options.rationale: Withholding steroids entirely is wrong because evidence (ACTORDS, MACS analyses) shows a rescue dose provides meaningful neonatal benefit when the prior course was over 7 days ago and birth is now imminent. Would be correct only if the original course were within the past 7 days (RANZCOG).

options.text: Give dexamethasone 6 mg IM as a single rescue dose
options.rationale: Dexamethasone 6 mg single dose is wrong because Australian dexamethasone dose is 12 mg per dose, and the preferred rescue agent is betamethasone 11.4 mg IM; the dose stated is half-strength. Would never match Australian rescue dosing (RANZCOG).

options.text: Withhold steroids until cervix is more than 5 cm dilated
options.rationale: Withholding until >5 cm is wrong because steroid foetal benefit takes hours to develop; the anti-pattern is treating steroid timing as a labour-progress decision rather than an anticipation-of-birth decision. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Rescue dosing is a single 11.4 mg betamethasone (or 12 mg dexamethasone) given before 34+0 when the original course was over 7 days previously and birth is anticipated within 7 days.
key_points: Rescue: single dose, not a repeat full course (RANZCOG)
Eligibility: prior course >7 days ago, gestation <34+0, birth likely within 7 days
Multiple repeats associated with lower birthweight
explanation.pearls: Document time of every steroid dose precisely; it drives later rescue decisions.

stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 at 29+4 with a singleton pregnancy has progressed in spontaneous preterm labour despite atosiban tocolysis. She is now 7 cm dilated, contracting every 2 minutes, and birth is expected within the next 2 hours. She has had a complete course of betamethasone 36 hours ago. She has no contraindication to magnesium sulfate, has normal renal function, and no neuromuscular disease. She has no antepartum haemorrhage, no rupture of membranes, no fever and no symptoms of pre-eclampsia. The CTG is reactive and maternal observations are normal. Her booking BMI was 23, her booking bloods were unremarkable and she has not received magnesium sulfate at any earlier point in this pregnancy. The unit routinely uses MgSO4 for neonatal neuroprotection in line with RANZCOG and Australian Antenatal Magnesium Sulphate Neuroprotection guidance. Birth is now genuinely expected within 1 to 2 hours given her rapid cervical progress.

data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, UEC normal (creat 60), CTG reactive

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan for foetal neuroprotection?

options.text: Commence intravenous MgSO4 loading and maintenance now, aim for at least 4 hours before birth
options.rationale: RANZCOG and SOMANZ recommend antenatal MgSO4 for neuroprotection at gestations under 30 weeks when birth is anticipated within 24 hours; ideally given at least 4 hours pre-birth.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection of the fetus

options.text: Do not give magnesium sulfate, as it is only indicated in pre-eclampsia for seizure prophylaxis
options.rationale: Saying MgSO4 is only for pre-eclampsia is wrong because the foetal neuroprotection role is established independently (ACTOMgSO4, Cochrane meta-analysis NNT ~50 to prevent CP at very preterm gestations). Would be correct only if neuroprotection benefit had not been demonstrated, which is now well established (RANZCOG; SOMANZ).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection of the fetus
Cochrane: Magnesium for neuroprotection of the preterm fetus

options.text: Give a single intramuscular dose of magnesium sulfate now for foetal neuroprotection
options.rationale: Single IM dose is wrong because the regimen for neuroprotection is IV loading (4 g over 20-30 min) then maintenance infusion (1 g/hr); IM dosing does not achieve the required serum levels. Would never be the correct route (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection of the fetus

options.text: Withhold magnesium sulfate because a complete course of antenatal corticosteroids has been given
options.rationale: Withholding magnesium because steroids were given is wrong because corticosteroids (lung maturation) and magnesium (neuroprotection) act on different pathways and have independent benefit; the anti-pattern is treating them as interchangeable. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection of the fetus

options.text: Defer magnesium sulfate now as the gestation has progressed beyond 28 weeks
options.rationale: Deferring at 29+4 is wrong because Australian guidance offers neuroprotection up to <30 weeks (with some centres extending to <32 weeks); 29+4 sits inside the window. Would be correct only at gestations beyond local cutoffs (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection of the fetus

explanation.summary: Antenatal magnesium sulfate reduces cerebral palsy at very preterm gestations. Australian guidance recommends use when birth is anticipated within 24 hours at under 30 weeks, ideally with at least 4 hours of infusion before birth.
key_points: Window: <30 weeks Australian standard; some centres <32 weeks (RANZCOG)
Loading dose 4 g IV over 20 to 30 minutes then 1 g/hour infusion
Monitor reflexes, RR, urine output, magnesium level if prolonged
explanation.pearls: Do not delay birth for MgSO4 if maternal or foetal compromise demands urgent delivery.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection of the fetus
Cochrane: Magnesium for neuroprotection of the preterm fetus

stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 at 28+2 presents with a sudden gush of clear fluid 2 hours ago, followed by ongoing leakage. She has no contractions, no fever, no abdominal tenderness, and the foetal heart is reassuring. Speculum confirms a pool of clear amniotic fluid in the posterior fornix and the cervix appears closed. There is no offensive odour. Maternal observations are normal. Her GBS status from a 26-week swab is negative. She is admitted for expectant management with corticosteroids, MgSO4, and close maternal-foetal surveillance.

data_table.Examination: Pool of clear AF on speculum, cervix closed, no contractions, abdomen non-tender, FH reassuring
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, CRP 6, MSU pending, GBS negative at 26 weeks

lead_in: Which antibiotic regimen is most appropriate to prolong latency?

options.text: Oral erythromycin 250 mg four times daily for 10 days
options.rationale: RANZCOG and the ORACLE I evidence support oral erythromycin in PPROM to prolong latency and reduce neonatal morbidity; co-amoxiclav is avoided due to increased necrotising enterocolitis risk.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg orally twice daily for 10 days
options.rationale: Co-amoxiclav is wrong because the ORACLE I trial demonstrated increased neonatal necrotising enterocolitis with co-amoxiclav in PPROM; it is explicitly avoided. Would be correct only outside PPROM (RANZCOG; ORACLE I).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 1.2 g six-hourly for 48 hours of treatment only
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin alone for 48h is wrong because it is intrapartum GBS prophylaxis, not a latency regimen, and does not provide the prolongation seen with macrolides; the anti-pattern is conflating GBS prophylaxis with PPROM latency therapy. Would be correct only as intrapartum GBS cover when in labour (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus in pregnancy

options.text: Oral azithromycin 1 g as a single stat dose for latency prolongation
options.rationale: Single-dose azithromycin is wrong because that regimen is used as adjunct prophylaxis at caesarean (CSection trial), not as PPROM latency cover; PPROM requires a 10-day macrolide course. Would be correct only as caesarean prophylaxis (RANZCOG).

options.text: No antibiotic prophylaxis unless overt chorioamnionitis later develops in this patient
options.rationale: Withholding antibiotics until chorioamnionitis is wrong because PPROM <34 weeks routinely receives latency antibiotics; the evidence base predates clinical infection. Would be correct only at term PROM, not preterm (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

explanation.summary: Erythromycin is the latency antibiotic of choice in PPROM under 34 weeks. Co-amoxiclav is contraindicated due to the NEC signal in ORACLE I. Antibiotics complement corticosteroids and MgSO4 in expectant PPROM management.
key_points: Erythromycin 250 mg QID x 10 days or until birth (RANZCOG, eTG)
Avoid co-amoxiclav (NEC risk)
Add GBS intrapartum prophylaxis once labour established
Monitor for chorioamnionitis: fever, tachycardia, uterine tenderness, FHR
explanation.pearls: Erythromycin allergy: clarithromycin is a reasonable alternative; check with senior.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes
RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus in pregnancy

stem: A 34-year-old G2P1 at 36+5 presents 3 hours after a gush of clear fluid. She has no contractions, the foetal heart is reassuring, and she is afebrile. Speculum confirms PROM with a pool of clear fluid; the cervix appears favourable. She is GBS positive on a 36-week swab. Her last birth was a spontaneous vaginal birth at term without complication.

data_table.Examination: Pool of clear AF, cervix favourable, no contractions, FH reassuring
data_table.Investigations: GBS positive at 36 weeks, FBC normal, CRP normal

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Plan induction of labour now with intrapartum benzylpenicillin for GBS
options.rationale: PPROM at or beyond 36+0 with a GBS-positive mother is managed by induction rather than expectant care to limit chorioamnionitis and neonatal early-onset GBS sepsis (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes
RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus in pregnancy

options.text: Expectant management at home for 96 hours with daily review
options.rationale: Outpatient expectant management is wrong because near-term PPROM with GBS positivity carries clear risk of chorioamnionitis and early-onset neonatal GBS sepsis, and outpatient observation prevents timely intrapartum penicillin. Would be correct only if she were GBS-negative and could tolerate close inpatient observation, not at home with GBS positivity (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus in pregnancy

options.text: Tocolysis with nifedipine and a course of corticosteroids
options.rationale: Tocolysis plus steroids is wrong because steroids are not routinely given beyond 35 weeks and tocolysis only prolongs membrane rupture while increasing infection; the anti-pattern is treating 36+5 like a much earlier preterm scenario. Would never be correct at this gestation (RANZCOG).

options.text: Caesarean section now due to PPROM beyond 36 weeks
options.rationale: Caesarean for PPROM at this gestation is wrong because PPROM is not a caesarean indication; vaginal birth after induction is the preferred route in the absence of other contraindications. Would be correct only with obstetric indications for caesarean (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Expectant management as an inpatient until 39+0 weeks
options.rationale: Inpatient expectancy to 39+0 is wrong because prolonging ruptured membranes with GBS positivity drives chorioamnionitis and neonatal sepsis; the TERMPROM trial and RANZCOG support expediting birth. Would be correct only with reassuring clinical picture and GBS negativity, perhaps to 37 weeks, not 39 (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

explanation.summary: At or beyond 36+0 with PPROM, prompt induction with appropriate intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis (penicillin if GBS positive) is preferred to expectant management to reduce neonatal sepsis and chorioamnionitis.
key_points: PPROM 36+0 or more: plan induction rather than expectant care (RANZCOG)
Use benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IV q4h intrapartum for GBS positivity
Steroids not routinely indicated 35+0 or more in Australia
explanation.pearls: Time of rupture is the clock that drives chorioamnionitis risk and antibiotic decisions.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes
RANZCOG: Maternal Group B Streptococcus in pregnancy

subtopic_detail: PPROM near term - expectant vs delivery

stem: A 30-year-old G3P0 attends preconception clinic having recently had a third painless mid-trimester loss at 19 weeks. The two prior losses were at 18 and 20 weeks; on each occasion she presented with bulging membranes and minimal contractions, and the cervix was widely dilated on speculum. Anatomy scans had been normal, there was no antepartum bleeding, and she has had no infections or instrumentation between pregnancies. She is keen to conceive again and asks how the next pregnancy will be managed.

data_table.Investigations: Previous losses at 18, 20, 19 weeks; anatomy scans normal; no infection or instrumentation

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan for her next pregnancy?

options.text: History-indicated cervical cerclage at 12 to 14 weeks of pregnancy
options.rationale: Three or more prior second-trimester losses or preterm births consistent with cervical insufficiency is the classic indication for history-indicated (elective) cerclage at 12 to 14 weeks per RANZCOG.

options.text: Vaginal progesterone 200 mg nightly from 16 weeks without cerclage
options.rationale: Vaginal progesterone alone is wrong because progesterone is indicated for asymptomatic short cervix on TVS or singleton prior preterm birth, not as a substitute for cerclage in three prior second-trimester losses. Would be correct only as adjunct, not as sole strategy in this history (RANZCOG).

options.text: Serial cervical length scans from 16 weeks with rescue cerclage if shortening
options.rationale: Serial scans with rescue cerclage is wrong because women with three prior mid-trimester losses meet criteria for history-indicated (elective) cerclage; ultrasound-indicated cerclage is the strategy for women with one prior preterm birth where the cervix may not shorten. Would be correct only with one prior preterm birth, not three losses (RANZCOG; RCOG cerclage guideline).

options.text: Pre-conception transabdominal cervical cerclage placement before next pregnancy attempt
options.rationale: Preconception transabdominal cerclage is wrong because TAC is reserved for failed transvaginal cerclage or anatomical features (very short cervix, extensive trachelectomy) precluding vaginal placement; not first-line. Would be correct after a failed vaginal cerclage in a previous pregnancy (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: Insertion of cervical pessary at 14 to 16 weeks of pregnancy
options.rationale: Cervical pessary is wrong because the evidence is mixed (PECEP positive, ProTWIN/OPPTIMUM neutral) and pessary is not first-line in Australian practice for this history. Would be correct only in research settings or specific resource-limited contexts (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Cervical insufficiency presents as painless mid-trimester loss with dilation and bulging membranes. Three or more such losses warrant history-indicated cerclage at 12 to 14 weeks in the next pregnancy, with subsequent cervical length surveillance.
key_points: History-indicated cerclage: 3+ prior mid-trimester losses / early PTB
Ultrasound-indicated cerclage: 1+ prior PTB and TVS cervix <25 mm before 24 weeks
Rescue cerclage: cervix dilated with intact membranes <24 weeks
explanation.pearls: Always exclude acute infection and contractions before placing rescue cerclage.

stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 at 22+0 with a singleton pregnancy attends for a planned TVS cervical length assessment because of one prior spontaneous preterm birth at 32 weeks. She has no symptoms today: no bleeding, no fluid loss, and no contractions. Anatomy scan was normal at 20+1. TVS today shows a cervix of 18 mm with no funnelling. Foetal fibronectin is not routinely used at her unit at this gestation. She has no contraindications to progesterone. Her previous birth was a spontaneous preterm vaginal birth at 32 weeks with no recognised infection, no antepartum bleeding and no overt cervical instrumentation. She has no other significant medical or obstetric history, takes no medications other than folate and iodine, and has no contraindications to vaginal progesterone. Her unit follows current RANZCOG guidance recommending vaginal progesterone in women with a singleton pregnancy and short cervix on TVS prior to viability.

data_table.Examination: No contractions, no PV bleed or fluid
data_table.Investigations: TVS cervix 18 mm, no funnelling, FH and AF normal

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate intervention?

options.text: Commence vaginal progesterone 200 mg nightly continued until 36 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Vaginal progesterone reduces preterm birth and perinatal morbidity in women with TVS cervix under 25 mm before 24 weeks, particularly with a prior preterm birth, per RANZCOG.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Use of progesterone to prevent preterm birth

options.text: Intramuscular 17-hydroxyprogesterone caproate weekly until 34 weeks gestation
options.rationale: IM 17OHPC is wrong because the PROLONG trial confirmed lack of efficacy and the FDA withdrew approval; vaginal progesterone has the supporting evidence (PREGNANT, OPPTIMUM, individual patient meta-analyses). Would never be correct in current Australian practice (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Use of progesterone to prevent preterm birth

options.text: Rescue cervical cerclage placement now via vaginal approach
options.rationale: Rescue cerclage is wrong because rescue cerclage is for advanced cervical dilation with exposed membranes; this woman has a closed short cervix. Would be correct only with dilated cervix and bulging membranes (RANZCOG).

options.text: Strict bedrest at home until 34 completed weeks gestation
options.rationale: Bedrest is wrong because trials have shown bedrest does not reduce preterm birth and increases VTE and muscle deconditioning; the anti-pattern is offering bedrest as a 'do something' intervention. Would never be correct (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Use of progesterone to prevent preterm birth

options.text: Foetal fibronectin testing now to guide subsequent management
options.rationale: qfFN testing is wrong because fFN has its strongest predictive role in symptomatic women; in an asymptomatic woman with short cervix the management trigger (progesterone) is already in place regardless of fFN. Would be correct only in symptomatic threatened preterm labour (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: TVS cervix under 25 mm before 24 weeks identifies women at high risk of preterm birth. Vaginal progesterone (200 mg nightly) reduces preterm birth and improves neonatal outcomes; cerclage is reserved for those with prior preterm birth whose cervix shortens despite progesterone or who present with dilation.
key_points: TVS cervix <25 mm before 24 weeks: vaginal progesterone (RANZCOG)
Continue progesterone until 36 weeks
Consider US-indicated cerclage if cervix <25 mm and prior PTB
explanation.pearls: TVS is the standard cervical length method; transabdominal underestimates true length.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Use of progesterone to prevent preterm birth

stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 at 29+2 attends pregnancy assessment with intermittent abdominal tightening over 8 hours and pelvic pressure. She has no bleeding, no rupture of membranes, no fever, and a reassuring CTG. Examination shows no fluid pooling on speculum and the cervix appears long and closed; TVS measures the cervix at 22 mm. Quantitative foetal fibronectin (qfFN) returns at 12 ng/mL. She has no obstetric history of preterm birth and no infections. She has no past history of preterm birth, no late miscarriage, no cervical surgery and no documented uterine anomaly. There is no antepartum bleeding, no offensive vaginal discharge and a urine dipstick is unremarkable. The maternity unit uses quantitative foetal fibronectin combined with TVS cervical length as the basis for triage of suspected preterm labour, with a published unit cut-off of 50 ng/mL above which admission and steroids are considered.

data_table.Examination: No pooling, cervix long/closed on speculum
data_table.Investigations: TVS cervix 22 mm, qfFN 12 ng/mL, CTG reactive, MSU pending

lead_in: Which management approach best reflects current evidence?

options.text: Reassure, discharge home with clear safety-netting advice and outpatient follow-up
options.rationale: qfFN below 50 ng/mL combined with a TVS cervix in the lower range still has a very high negative predictive value for birth within 7 days; admission, tocolysis, and steroids are unnecessary.
source_refs: NICE NG25: Preterm labour and birth

options.text: Admit, give a course of antenatal corticosteroids and commence tocolysis
options.rationale: Admit, steroids and tocolysis is wrong because qfFN <50 ng/mL with a closed cervix has very high NPV for birth within 7 days; admission and steroid courses are unwarranted. Would be correct only with positive qfFN or cervical change (RANZCOG).

options.text: Admit and commence prophylactic magnesium sulfate for foetal neuroprotection
options.rationale: Magnesium sulfate is wrong because neuroprotective MgSO4 requires birth anticipated within 24 hours, which the negative fFN explicitly refutes; the anti-pattern is using MgSO4 'just in case'. Would be correct only with imminent birth at <30 weeks (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection of the fetus

options.text: Repeat quantitative foetal fibronectin in 24 hours and admit until then
options.rationale: Repeat fFN in 24h is wrong because management does not change for a low qfFN value with no cervical change; the anti-pattern is using repeat testing as a substitute for clinical decision-making. Would be correct only if the clinical picture changed (RANZCOG).

options.text: Insertion of emergency rescue cervical cerclage now in theatre
options.rationale: Emergency rescue cerclage is wrong because cerclage requires advanced cervical dilation with bulging membranes, not a closed cervix with negative fFN. Would be correct only with cervical dilation and exposed membranes (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: qfFN under 50 ng/mL with reassuring TVS cervix has a very high negative predictive value for birth within 7 days. The greatest clinical value of qfFN is in safely discharging women with threatened preterm labour.
key_points: qfFN NPV for PTB within 7 days >95% when <50 ng/mL
Combine with TVS cervical length for best discrimination
Positive qfFN raises risk; manage clinically with steroids, MgSO4, tocolysis as indicated
explanation.pearls: Avoid vaginal exam, intercourse, or speculum lubrication before fFN sampling.

sources.label: NICE NG25: Preterm labour and birth
RANZCOG: Magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection of the fetus

subtopic_detail: Foetal fibronectin in threatened preterm labour

stem: A 31-year-old G1P0 at 37+1 with well-controlled gestational diabetes on diet alone is reviewed at her routine antenatal clinic. Foetal growth is on the 50th centile, amniotic fluid is normal, CTG is reactive, and self-monitored capillary glucose has stayed within target. She has no hypertension, no IUGR, and no other complication. She has booked into a private hospital and asks whether elective induction at 37 to 38 weeks would be safer than waiting.

data_table.Investigations: EFW 50th centile, AFI normal, BSL diary within target

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan?

options.text: Continue routine antenatal care with planned delivery around 40+0 to 40+6 weeks
options.rationale: ADIPS and RANZCOG support birth by around 40+6 for diet-controlled GDM with no complications; routine early term induction does not improve outcomes and increases neonatal respiratory morbidity.

options.text: Elective induction of labour at 37+0 weeks gestation now
options.rationale: Elective IOL at 37+0 is wrong because routine 37+0 induction in well-controlled diet-only GDM increases iatrogenic late preterm respiratory morbidity without improving stillbirth or shoulder dystocia outcomes; ADIPS recommends ~40+6. Would be correct only with insulin-treated GDM or comorbidities (ADIPS; RANZCOG).

options.text: Elective lower segment caesarean section at 38+0 weeks of gestation
options.rationale: Elective caesarean at 38+0 is wrong because diet-controlled GDM does not in itself indicate caesarean; macrosomia thresholds and obstetric indications drive mode of birth. Would be correct only with EFW >4500 g, prior caesarean or other indication (ADIPS; RANZCOG).

options.text: Planned induction at 39+0 with cervical ripening regardless of Bishop
options.rationale: Induction at 39+0 regardless of Bishop is wrong because diet-controlled uncomplicated GDM can be managed expectantly to around 40+6 per ADIPS; routine early induction is reserved for insulin-treated or complicated disease. Would be correct only for insulin-treated GDM or other complications (ADIPS; RANZCOG).

options.text: Defer delivery until 41+5 weeks with daily CTG from 39 weeks
options.rationale: Deferring to 41+5 is wrong because even diet-controlled GDM raises stillbirth risk beyond 41 weeks; the upper boundary in ADIPS is around 40+6. Would never be correct in GDM regardless of control (ADIPS).

explanation.summary: ADIPS guidance supports delivery by around 40+6 in uncomplicated diet-controlled GDM. Earlier elective birth is reserved for insulin treatment, macrosomia, poor control, or other complications. Late preterm and early term iatrogenic births carry meaningful neonatal respiratory morbidity.
key_points: Diet-controlled uncomplicated GDM: birth by ~40+6 (ADIPS)
Insulin-treated GDM: consider birth 38+0 to 39+0
Complicated GDM (poor control, IUGR, hypertension): individualise earlier
explanation.pearls: Avoid elective non-medical birth before 39+0 due to neonatal respiratory morbidity.

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 39+3 presents 2 hours after a gush of clear fluid followed by ongoing leakage. Not contracting; speculum confirms a pool of clear amniotic fluid and a closed soft cervix. CTG reactive. GBS swab at 36 weeks was POSITIVE. She has no penicillin allergy. Booking BMI 24, otherwise uncomplicated pregnancy. She asks about waiting at home for spontaneous labour; she has no childcare for her older child and would prefer to wait if it is safe.

data_table.Examination: Pool of clear AF on speculum, soft closed cervix, no contractions
data_table.Investigations: CTG reactive, GBS negative at 36 weeks

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan in this Australian setting?

options.text: Admit for immediate induction of labour and commence intrapartum IV benzylpenicillin GBS prophylaxis now
options.rationale: Term PROM with GBS-positive status: RANZCOG recommends immediate induction and commencement of intrapartum GBS prophylaxis (rather than expectant management) to reduce risk of early-onset neonatal GBS sepsis. Penicillin is first-line in non-allergic women (RANZCOG term PROM; RANZCOG GBS).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

options.text: Admit for expectant management up to 24 hours with no antibiotics unless fever develops
options.rationale: Expectancy with no antibiotics unless febrile is wrong because GBS-positive PROM warrants IAP from the time of presentation; waiting for fever signals fully established chorioamnionitis. Would be correct only in GBS-negative term PROM with stable observations (RANZCOG GBS).

options.text: Wait at home for spontaneous labour for up to 5 days with daily review
options.rationale: Home expectancy 5 days is wrong because outpatient management beyond 24h in term PROM raises chorioamnionitis risk and is incompatible with timely IV penicillin in a GBS-positive woman. Would never be correct in GBS-positive PROM (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

options.text: Mandatory immediate lower-segment caesarean to prevent ascending infection
options.rationale: Mandatory caesarean is wrong because vaginal birth with IAP is the standard approach in GBS-positive PROM; caesarean does not eliminate vertical transmission and adds maternal morbidity. Would be correct only for standard obstetric indications (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

options.text: Commence oral erythromycin to prolong latency to delivery at term
options.rationale: Erythromycin for latency is wrong because erythromycin latency therapy is for preterm PROM; at term, the goal is induction, not prolongation. Would be correct only at <34 weeks PPROM (RANZCOG; ORACLE I).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

explanation.summary: Term PROM (>=37+0) is managed by shared decision between prompt induction and limited expectant management up to around 24 hours, after which induction is recommended to reduce infection risk.
key_points: Either induction or expectant management for ~24 h is reasonable
GBS positivity tips management toward earlier induction with intrapartum penicillin
Document time of rupture
explanation.pearls: Avoid vaginal exams unless necessary before active labour to limit infection risk.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 at 29+0 is on day 9 of expectant PPROM management. She completed corticosteroids 7 days ago and erythromycin is ongoing. She has been afebrile with normal observations until this evening, when she reports increased uterine tenderness and chills. Observations: T 38.4, HR 118, BP 108/64, RR 18, SpO2 98 on RA. Her uterus is tender on palpation. CTG shows a baseline foetal heart rate of 175 bpm with reduced variability and no decelerations. WCC has risen to 18.6 x10^9/L, neutrophils 16.2 x10^9/L, CRP 92 mg/L (from 18 mg/L two days ago). She has no offensive vaginal discharge but the speculum view shows turbid amniotic fluid pooling. Maternal lactate is 2.1 mmol/L.

data_table.Examination: Uterine tenderness, turbid AF on speculum, no other localising findings
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.6, neutrophils 16.2, CRP 92 (up from 18), lactate 2.1, MSU pending, CTG: baseline 175, reduced variability

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence IV broad-spectrum antibiotics and expedite delivery now
options.rationale: Clinical chorioamnionitis in PPROM at 29 weeks is an indication for prompt delivery alongside IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, irrespective of gestation, per RANZCOG.
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Continue expectant management with IV antibiotics, aim to reach 32 weeks
options.rationale: Continued expectancy to 32 weeks is wrong because once clinical chorioamnionitis is established, prolonging pregnancy drives maternal sepsis and foetal injury (white matter injury, neonatal sepsis); delivery is the definitive treatment. Would never be correct in active chorioamnionitis (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Repeat antenatal steroids and reassess clinical status in 12 hours
options.rationale: Repeat steroids and wait 12h is wrong because steroid benefit takes hours and does not change outcome once intra-amniotic infection is overt; the anti-pattern is delaying delivery to complete steroid courses. Would be correct only in subclinical situations (which is not this case) (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Add tocolysis to allow safe transfer to a tertiary unit
options.rationale: Tocolysis is wrong because tocolysis is contraindicated in chorioamnionitis: it delays definitive treatment and may mask deterioration. Would never be correct in chorioamnionitis (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Amniocentesis for Gram stain and glucose before any decision
options.rationale: Amniocentesis first is wrong because the clinical diagnosis (fever, tachycardia, tender uterus, rising CRP, turbid AF) is already established; invasive testing only delays delivery. Would be correct only when subclinical infection needs confirmation and delivery is not yet planned (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

explanation.summary: Clinical chorioamnionitis (maternal fever, maternal tachycardia, foetal tachycardia, uterine tenderness, foul or turbid fluid, leukocytosis) ends expectant PPROM management. Treatment is IV broad-spectrum antibiotics and prompt delivery regardless of gestation.
key_points: Triad-plus signs of chorioamnionitis = deliver (RANZCOG, eTG)
Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics started immediately on suspicion
Mode of birth depends on obstetric factors; chorioamnionitis itself is not a caesarean indication
Continue antibiotics intrapartum and postpartum until afebrile
explanation.pearls: Foetal tachycardia is often the earliest objective sign of intra-amniotic infection.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Term prelabour rupture of membranes and preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 with an MCDA twin pregnancy at 27+4 presents with reduced foetal movements over 24 hours. Pregnancy has been uncomplicated; serial scans have shown concordant growth and no TTTS. Today's ultrasound confirms intrauterine demise of twin A with absent cardiac activity and small pericardial effusion. Twin B has a foetal heart rate of 148 bpm with reactive CTG, normal amniotic fluid, normal umbilical artery Doppler, and a peak systolic velocity in the middle cerebral artery of 1.8 multiples of the median. Maternal observations and coagulation profile are normal. She has no contractions and the cervix is 35 mm closed on TVS.

data_table.Examination: Soft uterus, no contractions, no PV bleeding
data_table.Investigations: Twin A demised. Twin B: HR 148, reactive CTG, UA Doppler normal, MCA-PSV 1.8 MoM. Maternal coags normal. Cervix 35 mm.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Urgent transfer to foetal medicine for assessment for foetal anaemia
options.rationale: MCA-PSV above 1.5 MoM in the surviving twin after MC co-twin demise suggests acute foetal anaemia from feto-foetal transfusion across shared anastomoses; intrauterine transfusion may reduce neurological injury (RANZCOG, ISUOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

options.text: Immediate lower segment caesarean section of the surviving twin at this gestation
options.rationale: Immediate caesarean is wrong because iatrogenic birth at 27+4 inflicts severe prematurity morbidity (RDS, IVH, NEC, ROP) and does not reverse hypoxic-ischaemic injury already sustained from the co-twin demise; intrauterine transfusion offers the chance to rescue the survivor and prolong gestation. Would be correct only after IUT fails or with further foetal deterioration (RANZCOG; ISUOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

options.text: Expectant management with weekly outpatient Doppler surveillance of the surviving twin
options.rationale: Weekly outpatient Dopplers is wrong because MCA-PSV >1.5 MoM is a time-critical red flag for foetal anaemia and requires same-day tertiary intervention, not weekly follow-up. Would never be correct with elevated MCA-PSV (RANZCOG; ISUOG).

options.text: Commence maternal intravenous heparin to prevent disseminated intravascular coagulation in mother
options.rationale: Maternal IV heparin is wrong because clinically significant maternal coagulopathy after single twin demise is rare in the short term, especially with normal baseline coagulation; the anti-pattern is treating maternal DIC reflexively. Would be correct only with documented developing coagulopathy (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

options.text: Selective foetal reduction of the surviving twin by ultrasound-guided cord occlusion
options.rationale: Cord occlusion of the survivor is wrong because the goal after MC co-twin demise is to rescue the surviving healthy twin; terminating it would be unethical and inverts the management logic. Would never be correct in this scenario (RANZCOG; ISUOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy

explanation.summary: After single twin demise in a monochorionic pregnancy, acute feto-foetal transfusion via placental anastomoses can cause severe anaemia in the survivor. MCA-PSV above 1.5 MoM signals foetal anaemia and warrants urgent foetal medicine assessment for in-utero transfusion. Iatrogenic preterm birth at 27 weeks rarely improves outcome and adds severe prematurity morbidity.
key_points: Surviving MC twin: ~15% risk of death, ~25% risk of neurological injury
MCA-PSV >1.5 MoM = significant risk of foetal anaemia (ISUOG)
Urgent neuroimaging (MRI) 4 to 6 weeks later if survival
Birth timing individualised; not routinely immediate
explanation.pearls: MCA-PSV is the same screening tool used in red cell alloimmunisation; same threshold.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy
